nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments including reef flats, lagoons, and upper reef slopes with moderate light and water movement

decorPreferences: several inches of spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Favia Coral draws the majority of its energy from zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae living within its tissue — and is therefore primarily photosynthetic. Supplemental target feeding once or twice per week has been reported to improve growth rate and color saturation; offer small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, zooplankton, micro-plankton, or small prepared coral pellets in the evenings when feeding tentacles are extended. Amino acid supplements have been reported to support polyp health and coloration.

goodWith: other LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (~7-10 cm)
aggressive SPS corals in direct contact

imageCandidates.notes: Oceanamazon, 2889x2552. Dipsastraea maxima under actinic/blue reef lighting showing the neon green-and-violet fluorescent polyp look hobbyists buy; sharp, colony fills frame — the strongest representation of the genus in the aquarium.
Diego Delso, 3838x3838. Favia (Dipsastraea) favus, Red Sea; crisp whole-colony in-situ dome with clear honeycomb corallites. Distinct species for genus variety.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x951. Dipsastraea pallida, whole rounded colony in-situ, sharp, honeycomb structure clearly visible. Distinct species.
Uploaded by Jean-Paul (CC0). Dipsastraea laxa, near-perfect spherical dome colony in-situ, sharp fine plocoid corallites. Distinct species.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x921. Dipsastraea matthaii, whole compact colony in-situ with distinctive raised ringed corallites. Distinct species.
Diego Delso, 2368x3552. Dipsastraea favus, lavender/violet growth form; high-res, sharp, shows intra-genus colour and shape variation versus the pink favus dome.
Philippe Bourjon, 1200x1013. Dipsastraea rotumana showing elongated meandering corallites; in-situ, distinct species — some sediment on colony but ID clear.
Haplochromis, 1599x1374. Favia sp. captive colony showing the cerioid meandering polyp structure aquarists see day-to-day; slightly pale but clean whole-colony framing.

summary: Favia Coral is a colorful large polyp stony coral widely regarded as one of the more forgiving LPS corals for beginners. It does well under moderate lighting and flow, benefits from weekly target feeding, and requires careful spacing from neighbors due to its sweeper tentacles.
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE: The name 'Favia' has remained in widespread use throughout the aquarium hobby despite a major molecular-based reclassification published in 2014. That revision moved the vast majority of Indo-Pacific species formerly placed in Favia to other genera, most prominently Dipsastraea, as well as Goniastrea, Coelastrea, and Favites. The genus Favia in its strict sense now contains only two Caribbean Atlantic species (F. fragum and F. gravida), which are not generally available in the US aquarium trade due to collection restrictions. The corals sold commercially as 'Favia' — including popular named morphs like Yellow Submarine, Wolverine, Mayan Sun, and Diversity Favia — are currently recognized as Dipsastraea sp. under the family Merulinidae (previously Faviidae).

CARE OVERVIEW: Favia corals are generally considered one of the more forgiving LPS corals in the reef hobby, making them suitable for beginners and intermediate aquarists alike. Their large, colorful fleshy polyps tend to adapt well to a range of tank conditions within the recommended parameter windows.

LIGHTING: Generally advised to keep under moderate light conditions, roughly 75–200 PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation, the measure of light energy usable by photosynthetic organisms). New specimens are generally best introduced to lower-light areas of the tank (50–100 PAR) and gradually acclimated upward over 2–3 weeks. Excessively high light (>200 PAR) has been reported to cause bleaching and color loss. LED, T-5, or metal halide lighting all have been reported to work well at appropriate intensities.

FLOW: Moderate water movement is generally recommended. Low-to-moderate flow prevents detritus from settling on the coral tissue and encourages healthy polyp extension. Strong direct laminar flow can stress the tissue and should be avoided.

PLACEMENT: Best placed on stable rockwork in the middle to lower portions of the aquarium. Favia corals are encrusting to dome-shaped and settle well on horizontal or angled surfaces. They have been known to extend sweeper tentacles 7–10 cm (approximately 3–4 inches) at night to sting neighboring corals; several inches of clear space around the colony is strongly recommended. Plan for moderate steady growth, roughly 2.5–7.5 cm per year under good conditions.

AGGRESSION: Favia corals are considered semi-aggressive, and careful spacing from all neighbors is important for peaceful long-term coexistence in a mixed reef.

WATER CHEMISTRY: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium dosing is generally advised for sustained skeletal growth. Elevated nitrate (>30–40 ppm) has been reported to affect coloration and polyp extension. Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–25 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.1 ppm) are tolerated, and some sources suggest slightly elevated nutrients improve coloration.

FEEDING: Favia corals are primarily photosynthetic, drawing most of their energy from zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae living within their tissue). Supplemental target feeding once or twice per week has been reported to noticeably improve growth rate and color saturation. Feed in the evening when feeding tentacles are visible; small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, zooplankton, powdered plankton, and prepared coral foods are all accepted.

PESTS: Flatworms are occasionally observed on Favia corals. Standard coral dips (e.g., CoralRx, Revive) are generally effective; new specimens should be dipped before introduction to prevent introducing hitchhikers.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) is the standard propagation method for Favia corals in captivity. Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a bandsaw; frags readily encrust on frag plugs or rubble and resume normal growth. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Favia Coral
primary.notes: Primary page returned 404; care parameters extracted from LiveAquaria search result content for the Maricultured Favia Coral listing (product 3291) and a CCGC Favia listing (6669), plus the crawled care summary text: temp 72–78°F, dKH 8–12, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, moderate lighting, moderate flow, semi-aggressive sweeper tentacles, feeding twice weekly in the evening.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Yellow Submarine Favia
additional.notes: Cross-checked all water parameters: temp 75–78°F, SG 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alk 8–9.5 dKH, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1300–1450 ppm, PAR 150–250, low-to-moderate flow, mid-to-lower placement, moderate care level. Parameters averaged with other sources.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Favia vs. Favites Blog & Product Pages
additional.notes: Cross-checked parameters: PAR 100–150, pH 8.0–8.4, alk 8–10 dKH, Ca 420–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1350 ppm, SG 1.024–1.026, feeding 3–4 times per week. Product pages confirm easy care, medium light, medium flow. Used for temperament and feeding guidance.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Favia Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic reclassification details (Favia to Dipsastraea 2014 split, Merulinidae family). Care data: PAR 50–100, low-to-moderate flow, bottom placement preferred, Ca ~425 ppm, Mg ~1350 ppm. PAR data on the conservative side; averaged with other sources to yield 75–200 range. Confirmed feeding 3x/week with mysis and powdered plankton.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Your Favia's Not a Favia
additional.notes: Used for reclassification documentation only: confirmed 2014 molecular reclassification moved most Indo-Pacific Favia to Dipsastraea; confirmed family Merulinidae (formerly Faviidae); confirmed true Favia sensu stricto now only F. fragum and F. gravida (Caribbean). Not used for husbandry parameters.
